body_txt: When a woman reigns, or otherwise holds some position of power or importance, she may do so with a masculine title and all of the authority that goes with it, instead of using the title's feminine equivalent. The most common version of this takes the trope name literally (a female monarch titled as "King" instead of "Queen"), but there are many other gender-specific titles that can be used instead. This might be because: This trope is frequently employed as a means of Gender-Concealing Writing . Gender Inversions (i.e. He Is The Queen) are far less common due to Men Are Generic, Women Are Special , but they do happen. It could be because the society is a matriarchy, the title refers to the king's spouse, or for some other reason. Note that a female character using a gender-neutral title is not this trope because that's what gender-neutral titles are for . Somewhat surprisingly, this trope only very rarely involves a transgender monarch. This also applies to traditional non-English titles, which are usually gendered even if they aren't obvious to the English speaker, like emir in Arabic, note in which all nouns have grammatical gender, and emir is inherently masculine; emira is the female equivalent or wáng in Mandarin Chinese. note which lacks grammatical gender but for historical/patriarchal reasons reserves wáng for men and further makes a distinction between wánghòu (王后, lit. "behind the king") for the wife of a reigning king, or a queen consort and nǚwáng (女王, lit. "female wáng "), a reigning queen or queen regnant In terms of the ranks of Authority Tropes , the tropes that are equal are God Save Us from the Queen! , The High Queen , The Good King , and President Evil . The next steps down are The Evil Prince , Prince Charming , Prince Charmless , Sheltered Aristocrat , Warrior Prince , The Wise Prince , and all Princess Tropes . The next step up is The Emperor . See also Matriarchy . For other unexpected titles of rulership, see Princesses Rule and Just the First Citizen . Don't Call Me "Sir" can arise when the ruler would prefer a feminine title. A Female Pope would also apply if the female pontiff insists on going by "Pope", which is derived from the Greek "πάππας", which means "pappa".
